lesson.title: Channels and Select
lesson.summary: Communicate between goroutines safely with channels.

sections.heading: Channels — communication between goroutines
sections.bodyHtml:
A channel is a typed conduit that lets goroutines send values to each other. Create one with make(chan Type):

ch := make(chan int)

// Send a value into the channel (in a goroutine)
go func() { ch <- 42 }()

// Receive a value from the channel
val := <-ch
fmt.Println(val)  // 42

The arrow indicates direction: ch <- sends, <-ch receives. Both block until the other side is ready — sends wait for a receiver, receives wait for a sender.

sections.heading: Buffered channels
sections.bodyHtml:
A buffered channel can hold a fixed number of values without a receiver. Sends only block when the buffer is full:

ch := make(chan string, 2)
ch <- "a"   // does not block
ch <- "b"   // does not block (buffer full after this)
// ch <- "c"  // would block until someone receives

fmt.Println(<-ch)  // "a"
fmt.Println(<-ch)  // "b"

Use buffered channels when you know the capacity upfront (e.g. collecting results from N goroutines).

sections.heading: Range and close
sections.bodyHtml:
You can iterate over a channel with range until it is closed. The sender calls close(ch) to signal no more values:

ch := make(chan int)
go func() {
    for i := 0; i < 5; i++ {
        ch <- i
    }
    close(ch)
}()

for n := range ch {
    fmt.Println(n)  // 0, 1, 2, 3, 4
}

Only the sender should close a channel. Sending on a closed channel panics.

sections.heading: Select — wait on multiple channels
sections.bodyHtml:
The select statement waits on multiple channel operations. It chooses one at random among those that are ready:

select {
case msg := <-ch1:
    fmt.Println("from ch1:", msg)
case msg := <-ch2:
    fmt.Println("from ch2:", msg)
case <-time.After(1 * time.Second):
    fmt.Println("timeout")
default:
    fmt.Println("none ready")
}

default runs immediately if no other case is ready. Use select with an empty default for a non-blocking read.

sections.heading: Your turn
sections.bodyHtml:
Open practice/channels/main.go.

Write a program that creates a goroutine sending numbers 1 through 5 on a channel, then reads and prints them in main.

questions.prompt: What happens when you receive from an unbuffered channel with no sender ready?
What does select do when multiple cases are ready?

options.label: Chooses the first case in source order

exercises.title: Channel send and receive
exercises.instructions: Send numbers 1-5 on a channel from a goroutine. Read and print them in main.
